summary:
The primary indication where submental intubation can be considered instead of tracheostomy involves mandibular fractures; however, there are also considerations extending this practice into more severe craniofacial trauma scenarios requiring preservation of anatomical structures during critical interventions.
Submental intubation offers benefits particularly when dealing with mandibular or certain types of facial bone fractures that necessitate minimizing invasive procedures while ensuring safe airway management without compromising structural stability crucially affected areas such as those involving major bony prominences including zygomatic arches etc.. To further refine focus:

1) Mandibular fracture scenario remains core application
2) Panfacial/complex injury context highlights broader relevance though less direct support exists currently from literature reviewed. Agent2 concludes focusing mainly on mandible-related indications yet acknowledges expanded use cases warranting additional research exploration regarding specific nuances across different kinds of maxillo-facial traumas managed via non-invasive means whenever feasible within clinical guidelines set forth based available evidentiary base presented herein. Summary: For mandibular fractures, submental intubation serves as a safer alternative to tracheostomy by reducing risk associated with mouth opening maneuvers after jaw reduction. In more extensive craniofacial injuries, although not definitively established through all referenced studies, submental intubation may offer comparable benefit by maintaining necessary anatomical structure integrity whilst providing secure ventilation options. 

This summary captures both main applications identified—primarily focused on mandibles—with acknowledgment towards potentially applicable situations encompassed under 'craniomaxillofacial' categories albeit lacking conclusive proof according initial document review process conducted previously mentioned above.
